keyPoints: Require mandatory reporting and dual-role training for all medical leaders and executives — because the Director of Medical Services' instruction to minimise documentation demonstrates that institutional leadership training has failed; executives must understand their own mandatory notification obligations and the governance liability of minimisation

zeroMarkTraps: Recommending only staff education without creating audit triggers, investigation pathways, or victim support structures.
Focusing only on the impaired clinician's wellness pathway without addressing the patient-victim support gap.
Failing to address how seniority and institutional hierarchy created an exemption from the standard response.
stems.postExaminerNote: High-yield governance answers create hard pathways with named roles and audit mechanisms: automated triggers with action pathways, dedicated patient-victim support, an impaired senior clinician process that is hierarchy-independent, and mandatory executive training verified by implementation audit.

title: Nabil — Fitness to Drive and Public Safety
case: Nabil, 44, schizoaffective disorder, long-distance coach driver, missed depot injections, persecutory beliefs about passengers, school excursion rostered next day
topic: Fitness to drive and public safety in a commercial coach driver with schizoaffective disorder, missed depot injections, persecutory beliefs incorporating passengers and the bus itself, and imminent school transport duties; tests application of national fitness-to-drive standards, confidentiality limits, reporting obligations, and refusal of misleading clearance letters under financial and family pressure.

stems.vignette: Nabil is a 44-year-old man with schizoaffective disorder who drives long-distance coaches. He attends for routine review after missing two depot injections. He says he is well and needs a clearance letter for work. His wife reports he has been sleeping poorly, talking about passengers being 'planted agents,' and checking under the bus for tracking devices. Nabil says, 'Driving calms me. I have never crashed.' He is rostered to drive a school group the next day.
stems.question:
Outline — list and justify your assessment of fitness to drive and public-safety risk, including reference to current national fitness-to-drive standards.
A list without because-clauses specific to this case will not be awarded any marks.
(10 marks | 10 minutes)
stemSignals: Commercial coach driver — a substantially higher safety standard than private driving applies because passengers and school children are exposed to the risk.
Missed depot plus relapse signs — current functional impairment, not historical diagnosis, is the clinical issue.
Passengers as 'planted agents' and checking bus for tracking devices — the driving environment itself is incorporated into the delusional content.
'Never crashed' — past crash-free history is not a current fitness assessment under any national standard.

domains.name: Fitness-to-drive standard and role-specific risk (4 marks)
keyPoints: Apply commercial-driver fitness standards under the Austroads National Driver Licensing guidelines rather than private-driver assumptions — because Nabil drives long-distance coaches and is rostered for a school group, so the applicable public-safety threshold is substantially higher than for a private licence holder

Assess current functional impairment, not historical diagnosis — because the clinical question is not whether Nabil has schizoaffective disorder but whether his current relapse symptoms, sleep deprivation and impaired reality testing affect safe commercial driving now

Recognise that the driving task is incorporated into the delusional content — because Nabil's belief that passengers are planted agents and his checking behaviour on the bus directly link active psychosis to the occupational driving environment; this is not background psychiatric history, it is current occupational risk

Decline to provide a clearance letter — because the current clinical evidence supports at least temporary suspension of commercial driving pending formal fitness assessment; a letter stating he is well and fit for work would be factually and clinically inaccurate

domains.name: Clinical relapse assessment (4 marks)
keyPoints: Assess psychosis and mood state systematically — because persecutory beliefs, checking behaviour, poor sleep and occupational paranoia together suggest an active relapse of schizoaffective disorder requiring formal mental state examination

Assess the functional impact of missing two depot injections — because missed depot in schizoaffective disorder creates a predictable and documented relapse pathway; restarting medication will not restore fitness immediately

Assess insight and realistic appreciation of driving risk — because Nabil's belief that driving calms him and his use of crash-free history as a fitness argument suggest impaired insight into the relevance of his current symptoms to occupational safety

Assess whether the current clinical picture indicates a need for involuntary assessment — because active schizoaffective relapse with persecutory beliefs about passengers, stated intent to drive a school group the next day, and refusal to accept clinical advice may engage involuntary assessment criteria

domains.name: Immediate public-safety risk (4 marks)
keyPoints: Treat tomorrow's school excursion roster as an immediate clinical and public-safety deadline — because the risk is not abstract or probabilistic; it is a specific, identifiable public-safety event involving children that is scheduled for the next morning

Assess risk of impaired driving behaviour from psychotic misinterpretation — because delusional interpretation of passengers as agents, checking behaviour under the bus, and paranoid vigilance during driving may impair attention, reaction time, decision-making, and behaviour toward passengers

Document the basis for the fitness assessment using national standards and specific clinical findings — because a refusal to provide a clearance letter in the context of a formal employment request creates medico-legal exposure; the clinical reasoning, the applicable standard, and the specific findings must be documented

Assess the medico-legal risk of issuing any misleading clearance letter — because a letter that misstates current fitness creates direct clinical, ethical and professional liability for the clinician who signs it regardless of the financial or employment pressure on the patient

zeroMarkTraps: Giving clearance on the grounds that Nabil has never had a crash.
Treating this as a routine relapse review requiring standard clinical management rather than an urgent commercial public-safety assessment.
Failing to identify tomorrow's school group roster as an immediate and specific safety deadline requiring action today.
stems.postExaminerNote: Candidates must recognise that commercial passenger driving with active persecutory symptoms incorporating the driving environment is a public-safety issue requiring immediate action, not employment support. The question asks for national standards; the answer must explicitly separate commercial and private driving fitness thresholds.

stems.vignette: The coach company says Nabil is rostered tomorrow for a school excursion. Nabil becomes angry when you advise he should not drive and says, 'You will ruin my life.' He refuses consent for you to contact his employer or licensing authority. He says he will drive anyway because he has a commercial licence.
stems.question:
Discuss — list and debate confidentiality, autonomy and public-safety obligations.
A list without debate structure will not be awarded any marks.
(10 marks | 10 minutes)
stemSignals: Refuses consent — the confidentiality conflict is now explicit and must be debated directly.
States he will drive anyway — voluntary clinical advice has failed; the public safety obligation becomes active.
School excursion tomorrow — imminent, identifiable, serious harm to children and the public.
Commercial licence — possession of a licence does not equal current medical fitness; this conflation must be explicitly addressed.

domains.name: Autonomy and confidentiality (4 marks)
keyPoints: Argument for respecting Nabil's autonomy and confidentiality — because employment, income, family stability and dignity are significant interests; disclosure without consent in an occupational setting should not occur without a genuine and specific safety threshold being crossed

Argument that confidentiality is not absolute when public safety is at imminent risk — because Nabil has active psychotic symptoms directly linked to the driving environment, has stated he will drive a school group despite medical advice, and the harm is imminent, serious and involves children who have not consented to the risk

Defensible position: the public-safety threshold has been crossed and confidentiality must yield — because the specific combination of active delusion involving passengers, stated intent to drive a school group the next day, and refusal of voluntary compliance places this unambiguously within the criteria for disclosure in the public interest

The commercial licence does not resolve the fitness question — because a licence is a legal permission granted at a point in time on the basis of medical fitness at that time; it does not create a current right to drive when a clinician has assessed the holder as medically unfit

domains.name: Reporting and notification pathway (4 marks)
keyPoints: Advise Nabil directly, clearly and specifically that he must not drive the school group — because clear, direct medical advice is a clinical and professional obligation before any disclosure; it preserves the therapeutic relationship and gives Nabil one final opportunity to comply voluntarily

Notify the relevant licensing authority through the applicable statutory pathway if he refuses to comply — because Nabil has declared intent to continue commercial driving while the treating clinician has assessed him as medically unfit; the licensing authority pathway exists specifically for this situation

Consider minimum necessary notification to the employer about tomorrow's roster — because the coach company controls who drives the school group tomorrow morning; if licensing notification cannot prevent harm by tomorrow, a minimum necessary notification to the employer may be required to remove Nabil from that specific roster

Assess whether the available statutory notification pathways under the relevant state legislation authorise or require reporting — because some Australian jurisdictions have specific provisions for treating doctors to notify licensing authorities when a patient has a condition impairing driving fitness; this pathway must be identified and applied correctly

domains.name: Clinical and safety management during consultation (3 marks)
keyPoints: Assess immediate escalation risk during the consultation — because anger, a stated belief that his life will be ruined, and active psychosis may create immediate self-harm, aggression or absconding risk that must be assessed and managed in the moment

Seek urgent senior, medicolegal and governance advice before disclosure — because overriding confidentiality in an occupational public-safety setting is a high-stakes decision with legal and professional consequences that requires documented senior consultation

Consider whether the acute presentation meets criteria for involuntary mental health assessment — because a person with active schizoaffective relapse and stated intent to drive a school group despite medical advice may be placing himself and others at risk in a way that engages involuntary assessment criteria

domains.name: Documentation (3 marks)
keyPoints: Document the clinical advice given, Nabil's refusal, the risk formulation and the disclosure decision in full — because this decision may be scrutinised by the licensing authority, employer, a court, AHPRA, or Nabil's family; the documentation must be contemporaneous, specific and complete

Document why less intrusive options were considered and found insufficient — because disclosure against consent requires a documented rationale naming the specific risk, the voluntary options that were attempted and failed, the applicable legal pathway, and the minimum necessary information disclosed
Document the medicolegal advice obtained and from whom — because a senior and external consultation that supports the disclosure decision is itself an important clinical governance record

zeroMarkTraps: Accepting Nabil's refusal of consent as the end of the clinical obligation without assessing whether the public-safety threshold for disclosure has been crossed.
Notifying only the licensing authority without considering whether tomorrow's school roster requires a separate and more immediate notification to the employer.
Disclosing Nabil's entire psychiatric history to the employer rather than minimum necessary safety information.
stems.postExaminerNote: This stem tests the threshold for disclosure without consent in a public-safety occupational setting. The best answer is proportionate and sequential: advise directly, seek consent, assess urgency, identify the applicable statutory pathway, then disclose minimum necessary information when imminent serious public risk remains. Panic disclosure and rigid non-disclosure are both wrong.

stems.vignette: Nabil's wife says loss of work will make the family homeless. His union representative requests a letter saying he is 'temporarily stressed but safe to drive with treatment.' Nabil agrees to restart depot medication but asks to continue driving until it works.
stems.question:
Describe — list and explain your immediate management, reporting and relapse-prevention plan.
A list without explanation will not be awarded any marks.
(10 marks | 10 minutes)
stemSignals: Financial hardship is real and requires active response — but it is not a reason to certify unsafe commercial driving.
Union requests a misleading letter — advocacy pressure is the clinical trap here.
Agrees to restart depot — treatment agreement does not equal immediate restoration of fitness.
Continue driving until it works — this framing is unacceptable as an interim risk management arrangement.

domains.name: Immediate management (4 marks)
keyPoints: Advise immediate cessation of commercial driving clearly and document this advice — explain that restarting depot medication does not immediately resolve persecutory symptoms, restore reality testing, or satisfy national commercial fitness-to-drive standards; the medication-trial period is not an interim during which commercial driving is safe

Restart treatment urgently and assess whether crisis or inpatient review is indicated — explain that missed depot, active psychosis, poor sleep, persecutory beliefs, and acute occupational distress together may indicate a relapse severity that exceeds ambulatory community management

Develop an immediate safety and crisis plan for Nabil and his wife — explain that loss of work, fear of homelessness, anger at the clinical recommendation and active psychosis create acute distress and possible self-harm risk that must be actively planned for

Assess whether the current presentation meets criteria for involuntary assessment — explain that if Nabil continues to refuse advice and indicates he intends to drive, the involuntary assessment pathway must be considered in parallel with the voluntary clinical plan

domains.name: Reporting and clearance letters (4 marks)
keyPoints: Refuse the union's proposed letter — explain that a letter stating he is safe to drive with treatment is clinically inaccurate because his current symptoms make him unsafe now regardless of treatment compliance; signing this letter creates direct professional liability

Provide only accurate factual documentation if any employment documentation is required — explain that a factual letter can state that Nabil is temporarily medically unfit for commercial driving pending clinical review and that he is receiving treatment; it must not contain a safety clearance

Complete licensing authority and employer notification as required — explain that active psychotic relapse, stated intent to drive, and refusal of clinical advice together require formal notification; the school excursion is an immediate public-safety event that the employer must be aware cannot proceed with Nabil as the driver

Clarify the medico-legal consequences of any misleading certificate to Nabil and the union — explain that a clinician who signs a clearance stating a patient is fit when they are not is exposed to professional sanction, civil liability and potential criminal liability; this is not a clinical preference but a professional obligation

domains.name: Relapse prevention and return-to-driving criteria (3 marks)
keyPoints: Set objective, named return-to-driving criteria — explain that Nabil must meet documented clinical criteria before any return to commercial driving: symptom remission confirmed by mental state examination, full depot adherence re-established, sleep normalised, insight demonstrated, and formal review against Austroads commercial standards completed

Increase monitoring intensity after depot restart — explain that recent non-adherence, occupational pressure and acute relapse indicate a high-relapse-risk period requiring more frequent clinical review, collateral from the wife, and a named escalation plan if any symptom return is identified before return-to-driving review

Engage the wife as a clinical partner in the monitoring plan with consent — explain that she identified the relapse signs first; a structured collateral monitoring arrangement with her is more sensitive to early relapse than fortnightly clinical review alone

domains.name: Social and financial supports (3 marks)
keyPoints: Actively address the homelessness and income risk rather than treating it as a background issue — explain that social work referral, employer sick leave entitlements, income support pathways, and financial counselling must be offered immediately; the family's financial risk is real and must be managed actively without substituting it for the safety decision

Clarify employment leave entitlements — explain that if Nabil is on sick leave rather than dismissed, the financial consequences may be less severe than the wife fears; providing this information actively may reduce the pressure the family is applying to the clinical decision

Ensure the wife understands the clinical reasoning and the clinical plan — explain that she is a key support person and has already shown she detects relapse; keeping her informed, with Nabil's consent, supports both the therapeutic plan and early relapse detection

zeroMarkTraps: Writing the union-requested clearance letter because the family is facing homelessness.
Allowing Nabil to continue commercial driving while waiting for depot medication to take effect.
Addressing the financial hardship by adjusting the safety determination rather than by actively engaging social supports in parallel.
stems.postExaminerNote: The high-yield answer refuses false reassurance while providing specific and active responses to financial harm. Safety is non-negotiable and is stated clearly; support is active, practical and addressed in parallel rather than treated as a reason to reconsider the clinical position.

title: Kyle — Intellectual Disability, Criminal Justice and Fitness
case: Kyle, 22, mild intellectual disability and ADHD, remand, assault charge, vulnerability in custody, fitness and community support planning
topic: Forensic assessment of a young adult with mild intellectual disability and ADHD on remand after assault charge; tests disability-informed assessment of cognitive and adaptive functioning, fitness to stand trial distinct from criminal responsibility, custodial safeguarding and exploitation risk, family involvement without infantilisation, and community forensic support planning.

stems.vignette: Kyle is a 22-year-old man with mild intellectual disability and ADHD who has been charged with assault after punching a security guard at a shopping centre. He is on remand because he breached bail by missing court. Custodial staff say he is 'childlike,' easily led by older prisoners, and keeps agreeing to things he does not understand. Kyle says, 'I just say yes so they stop talking.' His mother says he reads at a primary-school level and usually needs her help with appointments.
stems.question:
Outline — list and justify your assessment priorities in relation to intellectual disability, risk and custody.
A list without because-clauses specific to this case will not be awarded any marks.
(8 marks | 8 minutes)
stemSignals: 'I just say yes so they stop talking' — acquiescence and suggestibility are central to this assessment and may invalidate prior interviews and legal instructions.
Primary-school reading level — communication supports, accessible assessment methods, and legal comprehension assessment are required.
Easily led by older prisoners — custodial exploitation risk is an active safeguarding concern, not a background observation.
ADHD plus intellectual disability — impulsivity and adaptive functioning must be assessed, not IQ score alone.

domains.name: Intellectual disability assessment (4 marks)
keyPoints: Assess cognitive and adaptive functioning using disability-informed methods — because mild intellectual disability requires evaluation of reasoning, comprehension, practical literacy, daily living skills and support needs; a label alone without functional assessment does not support clinical or legal decision-making

Assess communication style and suggestibility as specific clinical priorities — because Kyle's statement that he says yes so people stop talking indicates acquiescence that may have invalidated prior police interviews, legal instructions, and statements given during custody

Obtain developmental, education and disability-service collateral — because mother's account, school records, prior NDIS or disability service assessments, and special educational history clarify Kyle's baseline function and provide evidence that cannot be obtained from clinical interview alone

Flag the suggestibility concern to the legal team before any further interviews proceed — because Kyle's acquiescence pattern may have compromised the reliability of prior statements; this must be communicated before police or legal teams conduct additional interviews that may further compound unreliable record

domains.name: Risk assessment (3 marks)
keyPoints: Assess the assault context through a disability-informed framework — because aggression in a person with mild intellectual disability and ADHD in a crowded public place may reflect sensory overload, misinterpretation of social cues, panic, prior victimisation, or impulsive response to threat rather than predatory violence

Assess risk of exploitation and retaliation within custody — because being described as childlike and agreeable by other prisoners creates both a current safeguarding emergency and a risk of being directed into harmful behaviour by others

Assess comorbid mental illness, trauma and anxiety independently of the intellectual disability — because depression, PTSD, anxiety and psychotic presentations can occur alongside intellectual disability; they are not excluded by the diagnosis and may be contributing to the current presentation and custodial behaviour

domains.name: Custodial safety and reasonable adjustments (3 marks)
keyPoints: Escalate custodial safeguarding concerns about exploitation immediately — because a person who says yes to stop people talking and who is being easily led by older prisoners is not a passive background risk; this is a current safeguarding emergency requiring active management

Request immediate reasonable adjustments in custody — because Kyle may need simplified explanations in all institutional communications, access to a support person for all formal interactions, protection from predatory prisoners, and disability-informed management by custodial staff

Screen for acute mental illness, neurodevelopmental comorbidity and custodial trauma — because intellectual disability and ADHD do not exclude depression, psychosis, anxiety responses or acute trauma reactions from being present and driving current behaviour

domains.name: Legal process support (2 marks)
keyPoints: Flag fitness and communication support needs to the legal team as a priority — because Kyle's comprehension deficits and acquiescence may impair meaningful participation in legal proceedings; this must be communicated before any further legal steps proceed

Clarify that Kyle's mother can provide practical support but should not be the sole interpreter of his wishes or instructions — because Kyle is an adult with supported decision-making rights; his mother's involvement must be structured to supplement his voice, not replace it

zeroMarkTraps: Treating mild intellectual disability as a complete explanation for the assault without assessing violence risk, context, and triggers.
Ignoring Kyle's vulnerability and exploitation risk because he is charged with assault.
Using his mother as sole interpreter of his wishes and instructions without directly assessing Kyle using accessible methods.
stems.postExaminerNote: Candidates should frame Kyle as simultaneously potentially vulnerable and potentially risky. Disability-informed practice is not the same as assuming innocence, incapacity or harmlessness. The acquiescence finding is the clinical hinge of this stem and must be flagged to the legal team.

stems.vignette: Kyle can name the charge but cannot explain what a plea is, thinks the judge is 'the boss of jail,' and believes his lawyer is 'the person who tells me what to say.' He becomes anxious when questions are abstract. The solicitor asks whether he is fit to stand trial and whether his disability means he lacked criminal responsibility. The legal criteria for mental impairment in the context of intellectual disability may differ from those applicable to psychotic mental illness.
stems.question:
Describe — list and explain how you would assess fitness to stand trial and distinguish it from mental state, intellectual disability, or responsibility at the time of the alleged offence.
A list without explanation will not be awarded any marks.
(10 marks | 10 minutes)
stemSignals: Can name the charge but not explain a plea — partial knowledge does not equal fitness; the criteria require functional understanding, not just recognition.
Judge as 'boss of jail' — specific role misunderstanding with clear functional implications.
Lawyer tells me what to say — inability to instruct counsel meaningfully is the most critical fitness criterion impairment.
ID-specific legal caveat — criteria for mental impairment involving intellectual disability may differ jurisdictionally from those for psychotic illness.

domains.name: Fitness as a current functional legal capacity (4 marks)
keyPoints: Assess fitness as current functional legal capacity, not as a diagnosis — explain that fitness concerns Kyle's present ability to participate meaningfully in proceedings; intellectual disability is the background context, but the fitness conclusion must rest on whether the specific functional legal criteria are met

Apply the fitness criteria concretely to Kyle's demonstrated deficits — explain that I assess whether he understands the charge, can explain a plea, understands the roles of court personnel, can follow trial evidence, can instruct his lawyer, and can make a defence; Kyle's misunderstanding of the judge's role and his belief that the lawyer tells him what to say indicate functional impairment of the counsel-instruction and court-personnel-understanding criteria

Use accessible assessment methods to distinguish genuine impairment from test anxiety or unfamiliarity — explain that simple language, visual aids, teach-back methods, short questions with breaks, and re-phrasing are required to ensure the assessment reflects Kyle's actual understanding rather than his anxiety or compliance responses

Assess whether communication supports could restore or enable fitness — explain that if Kyle's deficits are partly remediable through an intermediary, disability advocate, or structured legal preparation, an unqualified unfitness opinion without considering this is premature and may not serve his legal interests

domains.name: Communication and reasonable adjustments in assessment (2 marks)
keyPoints: Use the teach-back method specifically to distinguish genuine understanding from acquiescent agreement — explain that asking Kyle to confirm understanding will produce a yes from his acquiescence pattern; teach-back, where he explains concepts back in his own words without prompting, is the only reliable method for assessing actual comprehension in this case

Assess whether acute custodial factors are compounding the baseline cognitive limitation — explain that acute anxiety, sleep deprivation, fear of consequences, and possible trauma responses in custody may add reversible impairment to Kyle's baseline; a fitness assessment under these conditions may not represent his capable baseline and should consider a period of clinical stabilisation before final opinion

domains.name: Distinguish intellectual disability from acute mental illness (2 marks)
keyPoints: Distinguish stable baseline cognitive limitation from any acute, reversible overlay — explain that intellectual disability is developmental and stable, whereas depression, anxiety disorder, PTSD, psychosis or substance intoxication may add reversible cognitive impairment that changes both the fitness picture and the treatment plan

Do not equate mild intellectual disability with automatic unfitness — explain that some individuals with mild intellectual disability are fit to plead with appropriate supports, while others are not; the functional assessment against the legal criteria, not the diagnostic label, determines the conclusion

domains.name: Criminal responsibility at the time of the alleged offence (2 marks)
keyPoints: Separate fitness assessment from criminal responsibility clearly and explain the distinction to the solicitor — explain that fitness is a present-tense question about current participation capacity; responsibility is retrospective and concerns Kyle's state of mind, understanding, intent and self-control specifically at the moment of the alleged assault

Acknowledge the intellectual disability-specific legal caveat directly — explain that the legal criteria for mental impairment in the context of intellectual disability may differ from those applied to psychotic illness in the relevant jurisdiction; this requires specific jurisdiction-based forensic legal advice and I must be explicit about the limits of my clinical opinion on this question

zeroMarkTraps: Concluding Kyle is fit because he can name the charge, without applying the functional criteria to the demonstrated deficits.
Concluding Kyle is automatically unfit because he has intellectual disability, without applying the functional criteria.
Applying psychosis-based criminal responsibility reasoning without acknowledging that the criteria may differ for intellectual disability and require jurisdiction-specific forensic legal advice.
stems.postExaminerNote: The key examiner phrase is: fitness is current and functional; responsibility is retrospective and offence-specific. Intellectual disability informs both but does not automatically determine either. The acquiescence finding must appear in the assessment methodology, and the ID-specific legal caveat must be explicitly named.

stems.vignette: Kyle reports being pressured by other prisoners to give away his food and medication. He has bruises but says he 'fell.' His mother wants him home and says she can supervise him, but police say he has previously become aggressive when overwhelmed in crowded places.
stems.question:
Discuss — list and debate custodial safety, family involvement, public safety and least restrictive care.
A list without debate structure will not be awarded any marks.
(8 marks | 8 minutes)
stemSignals: Food and medication being given away under pressure — exploitation and medication diversion are active safeguarding concerns requiring immediate escalation.
Bruises but says he fell — possible assault requiring investigation; fear of disclosure is a safeguarding indicator.
Mother wants him home — a genuine protective factor that must be properly scoped.
Aggressive when overwhelmed in crowds — specific, situational public-safety risk that must be named and planned for, not dismissed.

domains.name: Custodial safety and safeguarding (3 marks)
keyPoints: Argument that custody is currently unsafe for Kyle — because he is being pressured for food and medication, has unexplained bruising, is suggestible with older prisoners, and lacks the social assertiveness to protect himself; this is an active exploitation and physical safety emergency

Argument that custody may remain necessary if community risk cannot be managed safely — because assault charges and documented history of aggression in crowded places represent a public-safety concern that cannot be dismissed simply because custody is proving harmful

Escalate the custodial safeguarding concerns immediately regardless of the release decision — because the bruising, medication diversion, and food pressure require active investigation and protective action in custody regardless of what the court ultimately decides about bail

domains.name: Family involvement (3 marks)
keyPoints: Argument for using mother's involvement as a genuine and significant protective factor — because she understands his literacy level, appointment needs, baseline functioning and communication style; her involvement materially improves the likelihood that any community plan is actually followed

Argument that mother's involvement must be scoped to support Kyle's adult autonomy, not substitute for it — because Kyle has expressed wanting to be treated as an adult; supported decision-making requires that his preferences and instructions are sought directly and documented, with the mother's role defined as facilitating rather than replacing his voice

Clarify the communication protocol with both Kyle and his mother before any release plan is finalised — because the tension between her wanting control and his wanting autonomy must be resolved with a specific, written, agreed arrangement rather than left to be negotiated in practice

domains.name: Public safety and least restrictive care (3 marks)
keyPoints: Argument for least restrictive release with disability-specific supports — because remand is causing active harm to Kyle through exploitation, and disability-informed community supervision with court attendance supports may manage risk more humanely and effectively than continued custody

Argument for structured release conditions rather than unstructured home placement — because prior aggression when overwhelmed in crowded places is a specific situational risk that must be addressed with specific avoidance and management strategies; sending him home without a crowd-trigger plan is not least restrictive care, it is inadequate planning

Defensible position: release is appropriate only if the support plan is specific and operationally enforceable — because least restrictive care is not equivalent to releasing Kyle without a plan; it requires that the supports, restrictions, monitoring and breach responses are each named, agreed, and verifiable

domains.name: Safeguarding documentation (2 marks)
keyPoints: Escalate bruising and possible assault through prison health and custody management channels — because unexplained bruising in a person who is being pressured and who demonstrates a pattern of acquiescent denial requires a formal safeguarding investigation, not a notation in the clinical file

Document the medication diversion concern and initiate a monitored administration plan — because Kyle giving away his medication under pressure may indicate both exploitation and non-adherence that creates clinical risk; medication must be administered under supervision in custody until this is resolved

zeroMarkTraps: Releasing Kyle simply because his mother offers supervision without specifying what the supervision consists of and how the known risk triggers are managed.
Keeping Kyle in unsafe custody without taking any active safeguarding steps to address exploitation and bruising.
Invoking least restrictive care to dismiss or minimise the specific, situational public-safety risk documented by police.
stems.postExaminerNote: The answer must hold vulnerability and risk simultaneously. The examiner rewards a balanced plan that protects Kyle from custodial harm while not minimising the specific, situational violence risk, and that uses supported decision-making to involve his mother without replacing his adult voice.

stems.vignette: The court asks what supports would be required if Kyle were released to the community while proceedings continue. His mother wants all communication to go through her, but Kyle says he wants to 'be treated like an adult.'
stems.question:
Outline — list and justify a community forensic support and recidivism-prevention plan.
A list without because-clauses specific to this case will not be awarded any marks.
(6 marks | 6 minutes)
stemSignals: Court asks for supports — an operational plan with specific components is required, not general disability advocacy.
Mother wants all communication through her — supported decision-making versus infantilisation is the explicit tension.
Kyle wants adult treatment — his preference must be heard and given weight in the plan.
Recidivism prevention must target overwhelm in crowds, missed court attendance, and custodial exploitation risk.

domains.name: Supported legal participation (3 marks)
keyPoints: Arrange a communication support person or legal intermediary for all court appearances — because Kyle's reading level, acquiescence pattern, and difficulty with abstract questions require an independent communication facilitator who is not his mother; this is a specific, named structural requirement for legal participation

Establish a court attendance support system with reminders and transport — because Kyle's bail was revoked for missing court; this is the specific failure the plan must address; a named person responsible for appointment reminders, accessible calendar support, and transport ensures this does not recur

Use supported decision-making rather than substitute decision-making in all interactions — because Kyle is an adult whose wishes and instructions must be sought directly; his mother's role is to provide communication support and practical assistance, not to speak on his behalf or control information flow

domains.name: Recidivism prevention plan (3 marks)
keyPoints: Create a specific crowd-trigger and overwhelm management plan — because the prior assault occurred when Kyle was overwhelmed in a crowded public place; the plan must name specific high-risk environments, avoidance strategies, grounding strategies, and a support person protocol for unavoidable crowded settings

Treat ADHD and behavioural regulation needs as a clinical priority — because impulsivity, poor planning, difficulty anticipating consequences, and emotional dysregulation are ADHD features that respond to treatment and are directly relevant to recidivism risk

Define mother's role clearly with Kyle's agreement — because she can appropriately provide appointment reminders, logistical support, and collateral to the clinical team; she should not control all clinical communication or replace Kyle's instructions; this boundary must be written into the plan and agreed by both

zeroMarkTraps: Routing all communication through mother despite Kyle's explicit request to be treated as an adult.
Writing a generic disability support plan without addressing court attendance, crowd triggers, and the specific recidivism risk.
Ignoring that the reason bail failed was missed court — the plan must specifically address this.
stems.postExaminerNote: The final stem rewards operational specificity: named communication support, reminders and transport, teach-back based legal preparation, crowd-trigger management, ADHD treatment, mother as supporter not substitute, and clear review and breach pathways. Generic disability advocacy does not achieve the standard.

title: Emily — Adult ADHD Assessment and Prescribing Governance
case: Emily, 29, solicitor, adult ADHD assessment, outpatient
topic: Adult ADHD diagnostic assessment in a high-achieving professional; tests longitudinal diagnostic reasoning, interpretation of collateral absence and non-prescribed stimulant use, multimodal treatment planning with prescribing safeguards, and managing GP and employer pressure before assessment is complete

stems.vignette: Emily is a 29-year-old solicitor referred by her GP for assessment of worsening concentration, missed deadlines, emotional reactivity and work-related distress. She was recently promoted to a role requiring independent management of large files and has been placed on a formal performance plan. The GP referral asks whether this is burnout, anxiety or adult ADHD. Emily says she has always felt chaotic internally but previously coped by working late and over-preparing. She brings screenshots of online ADHD screening questionnaires but also says she does not want another wrong label and just needs to understand why she is failing now.
stems.question:
Outline — list and justify the key information you would obtain to establish diagnostic confidence for adult ADHD and distinguish it from alternative explanations in this presentation.
A list without because-clauses specific to this case will not be awarded any marks.
(8 marks | 8 minutes)
stemSignals: The GP referral frames this as three alternatives — burnout, anxiety or ADHD — but all three can coexist; the assessment task is to establish which is primary and how they interact, not to pick one

Emily's self-referral with screening questionnaires and urgency around job preservation creates diagnostic pressure; the examiner trap is accepting her framing rather than running an independent assessment
High achievement at work does not exclude ADHD — but it does require explanation; if she coped until promotion, what changed and why?

domains.name: Diagnostic Reasoning (8 marks)
keyPoints: Clarify whether ADHD symptoms began in childhood — because adult ADHD is a neurodevelopmental disorder requiring a longitudinal pattern, not a new-onset response to workplace stress
Establish cross-setting impairment — because impairment limited to one high-pressure work role after promotion may reflect role mismatch or burnout rather than ADHD

Explore developmental and educational history including school reports where available — because contemporaneous accounts of disorganisation can confirm childhood onset even without parental collateral
Screen for comorbid generalised anxiety, depression, trauma, bipolar spectrum illness and sleep disorder — because each can cause inattention and emotional reactivity and changes the treatment plan
Take a detailed substance-use history including alcohol and cannabis — because both impair attention and affect prescribing safety independently of any ADHD diagnosis
Ask about previous psychiatric assessments, diagnoses and medication trials — because a history of prior labels or treatments may reveal diagnostic drift or confirm longstanding difficulties

Seek collateral history from a consenting source — because self-report in a high-stakes occupational context may amplify or minimise symptoms and external corroboration strengthens diagnostic confidence

Clarify Emily's occupational role demands and the specific nature of her performance failure — because understanding what changed at promotion helps distinguish ADHD unmasked by complexity from burnout or an anxiety disorder driven by role expectations

Assess Emily's goals and expectations from this assessment — because her urgency around medication must not substitute for diagnostic rigour, and her expectations shape therapeutic alliance and treatment planning

zeroMarkTraps: Accepting the GP's three-option framing without running an independent differential diagnostic process
Treating Emily's online ADHD screening scores as diagnostic evidence
Beginning with a treatment plan before diagnostic reasoning is complete
stems.postExaminerNote: Candidates who framed the assessment as establishing longitudinal evidence across settings rather than confirming a self-referred diagnosis, who explicitly named comorbidities that could explain the presentation, and who addressed the prescribing-pressure context as a reason for thorough rather than expedited assessment scored in the upper range. Candidates who organised their answer around the GP's three options, or who began planning treatment before establishing the diagnosis, did not achieve the standard.

stems.vignette: Emily becomes distressed when you ask about childhood collateral. She says her parents were emotionally abusive and that contacting them would be harmful. She performed well academically at school but recalls being described as bright but disorganised. She also admits she recently took one tablet of a friend's methylphenidate and felt calm and normal for the first time.
stems.question:
Discuss — list and debate how you would interpret the absence of easy parental collateral, her strong academic history, and her reported response to methylphenidate in forming a diagnostic position.
A list without debate specific to this case will not be awarded any marks.
(8 marks | 8 minutes)
stemSignals: The absence of parental collateral is a clinical problem to solve — not a reason to abandon diagnostic assessment or a reason to accept self-report unchallenged

High academic achievement creates genuine diagnostic tension: it can mask ADHD through compensatory effort and perfectionism, or it can indicate that ADHD is not the primary explanation for current impairment
Methylphenidate response is not a diagnostic test — stimulants improve focus in people without ADHD; the non-prescribed use itself raises safety and boundary concerns that must be addressed

domains.name: Formulation and Diagnostic Debate (8 marks)
keyPoints: Parental collateral is useful but not mandatory — because the diagnostic requirement is evidence of childhood onset and cross-setting impairment, not parental testimony specifically; this must be sought from alternative sources

Alternative collateral sources should be actively explored — because school reports, old GP records, a consenting sibling, partner observations or structured retrospective rating tools can provide childhood-onset evidence without requiring contact with abusive parents

Strong academic history does not exclude ADHD — because intelligent individuals often compensate through overwork, perfectionism and hyperfocus, masking ADHD until complexity or workload exceeds compensatory capacity

Strong academic history also genuinely weakens diagnostic confidence — because ADHD requires demonstrated impairment in childhood, not just subjective chaos; high grades achieved without apparent struggle require explanation before ADHD is confirmed
The methylphenidate response should be treated as one data point among many — because stimulants improve attention and reduce anxiety in people without ADHD; feeling calm is not pathognomonic

The non-prescribed stimulant use raises immediate prescribing safety and boundary concerns — because it signals potential for misuse or diversion, models medication-seeking behaviour, and must be named as a factor that increases the need for careful prescribing safeguards

The most defensible diagnostic position is to delay stimulant prescribing until assessment is complete — because premature confirmation of ADHD based on self-report, absent parental collateral and stimulant response creates iatrogenic risk

Anxiety, burnout, and trauma-related inattention remain live differentials — because emotional abuse history may produce hypervigilance, concentration difficulties and emotional reactivity that closely mimic ADHD without meeting neurodevelopmental criteria

zeroMarkTraps: Treating the methylphenidate response as confirmatory of ADHD
Concluding that ADHD cannot be diagnosed without parental collateral without exploring alternative sources
Ignoring the prescribing safety implications of non-prescribed stimulant use
stems.postExaminerNote: Candidates who articulated both sides of the academic history and methylphenidate response debates, named the prescribing safety concern raised by non-prescribed use, and took a defensible consultant position of completing assessment before prescribing scored in the upper range. Candidates who treated stimulant response as diagnostic or who dismissed diagnostic possibility because parental collateral was unavailable did not achieve the standard.

stems.vignette: After two assessment sessions, you find evidence of longstanding inattentive symptoms from childhood, current impairment at work and home, and significant comorbid generalised anxiety. There is no history of mania or psychosis. Baseline blood pressure, pulse and ECG are normal. Before your report is completed, the GP emails asking whether they can start stimulant prescribing to prevent job loss. Emily's employer also requests a certificate stating whether she is fit to continue in a role involving client trust accounts and legal deadlines.
stems.question:
Describe — list and explain a multimodal initial management plan, including treatment sequencing, prescribing governance, monitoring, non-pharmacological interventions and your response to the GP and employer requests.
A list without explanation will not be awarded any marks.
(11 marks | 11 minutes)
stemSignals: Two external parties are now applying prescribing and disclosure pressure — the GP wanting to start stimulants and the employer wanting a fitness certificate; both requests must be handled as distinct clinical and ethical problems, not administrative tasks
Comorbid generalised anxiety changes the stimulant prescribing sequence — anxiety may worsen on stimulants and must be treated actively as part of the plan, not as an afterthought

The employer's request touches on occupational disclosure and client-safety obligations — Emily's role involves managing client trust accounts and legal deadlines; there is a professional responsibility dimension that goes beyond standard workplace accommodation

domains.name: Treatment Sequencing and Prescribing Governance (5 marks)
keyPoints: Inform the GP that stimulant prescribing should not commence before the specialist report and documented monitoring plan are complete — because prescribing before the assessment is finalised bypasses the safeguards that protect Emily from misdiagnosis and adverse effects
If stimulants are indicated, begin with a long-acting preparation at the lowest effective dose — because this reduces misuse potential and minimises peak-trough anxiety and cardiovascular effects

Establish single prescriber and single pharmacy arrangements from the outset — because Schedule 8 stimulant prescribing requires governance safeguards that must be set up before prescribing begins, not added reactively
Treat generalised anxiety concurrently and actively — because untreated anxiety may worsen on stimulants and is independently impairing; sequencing must address both conditions

Arrange early clinical review during titration — because adverse effects including worsening anxiety, insomnia, appetite suppression and cardiovascular changes may emerge rapidly and require dose adjustment

domains.name: Monitoring (2 marks)
keyPoints: Establish baseline and ongoing monitoring of blood pressure, pulse, weight, sleep, appetite and anxiety severity — because cardiovascular and metabolic parameters must be tracked throughout stimulant titration and long-term prescribing
Screen for stimulant misuse, cannabis and alcohol use at baseline and during treatment — because ongoing substance use interacts with stimulant safety and may signal emerging misuse

domains.name: Non-Pharmacological Interventions (2 marks)
keyPoints: Offer ADHD-specific cognitive behavioural strategies, executive functioning coaching or structured ADHD skills work — because non-pharmacological approaches address planning, organisation and emotional regulation that medication alone does not fully treat

Provide psychological treatment for generalised anxiety — because CBT for anxiety has independent evidence and may reduce the stimulant dose required or clarify whether anxiety is the primary driver of impairment

domains.name: GP and Employer Communication (2 marks)
keyPoints: Respond to the GP collegially but clearly — explain that stimulant prescribing before the specialist report creates clinical and legal risk, and provide a timeline for the completed assessment and prescribing plan rather than a blanket refusal

Respond to the employer's fitness certificate request only with Emily's informed consent — explain that any certificate must be functional rather than diagnostic, limited to what Emily has consented to share, and must not disclose her diagnosis or treatment plan without her explicit agreement

zeroMarkTraps: Starting stimulants to prevent job loss before the assessment report is complete
Providing the employer with a diagnostic certificate without Emily's informed consent
Treating anxiety as a minor comorbidity rather than a condition that directly affects treatment sequencing
stems.postExaminerNote: Candidates who named the sequencing problem created by comorbid anxiety, addressed both the GP and employer requests as distinct clinical and ethical problems, and built prescribing governance into the plan from the start scored in the upper range. Candidates who prescribed to prevent job loss, ignored anxiety in the treatment plan, or provided employer disclosure without consent did not achieve the standard.

title: Thomas — Online ADHD Diagnosis and Shared-Care Refusal
case: Thomas, 35, online ADHD diagnosis, public community clinic, GP shared-care request
topic: GP shared-care stimulant prescribing request following inadequate online ADHD assessment; tests evaluation of a clinically deficient private report, managing an escalating patient under complaint threat with registrar present, and reformulating after undisclosed bipolar disorder and substance use emerge

stems.vignette: A GP sends an urgent referral asking the public adult community mental health service to take over stimulant prescribing for Thomas, a 35-year-old man diagnosed with ADHD by an online commercial clinic after a single brief video appointment. The attached report lists DSM symptoms and recommends dexamphetamine. It contains no collateral history, no physical health baseline, no substance-use history and no mood-disorder screening. The GP writes that they are not comfortable initiating Schedule 8 medication on this basis and that Thomas is angry and says the GP is obstructing his care.
stems.question:
Outline — list and justify the clinical, ethical and shared-care issues raised by this referral before any clinical decision is made.
A list without because-clauses specific to this referral will not be awarded any marks.
(8 marks | 8 minutes)
stemSignals: The GP's discomfort is clinically appropriate and should be supported — the examiner trap is treating the GP's caution as an obstacle to care rather than a defensible prescribing boundary
Thomas's anger at the GP is a clinical signal, not a reason to expedite prescribing — the consultant must assess what drives the anger before responding to it
The report's omissions are not minor gaps — they are the specific information required to prescribe stimulants safely; each missing element has a direct patient-safety consequence

domains.name: Clinical Issues (3 marks)
keyPoints: The report lacks collateral history, developmental assessment and cross-setting impairment data — because these are the core diagnostic requirements for adult ADHD and their absence means diagnostic confidence cannot be established from this document alone
The report contains no mood-disorder screening — because bipolar disorder is a contraindication to stimulant prescribing and must be excluded before Schedule 8 medication is initiated

The report contains no substance-use history or physical health baseline — because both are prerequisites for safe stimulant prescribing and their absence means cardiovascular and misuse risks have not been assessed

domains.name: Ethical Issues (3 marks)
keyPoints: A consultant cannot ethically endorse Schedule 8 prescribing based on a report they have not independently verified — because professional and legal responsibility for the prescribing decision rests with the prescribing clinician, not the referring service

The GP's refusal to prescribe is professionally appropriate and should be supported — because the GP is correctly identifying that the available information does not meet the standard for safe initiation

Thomas's anger and complaint threat cannot override clinical safety — because prescribing to avoid a complaint is a recognised ethical failure and creates greater long-term risk to the patient and the prescriber

domains.name: Shared-Care Issues (2 marks)
keyPoints: Shared care requires a defined agreement about diagnosis, initiation, monitoring, dose changes and escalation — because without a written shared-care agreement, responsibility is unclear and patient safety is compromised

The immediate response should offer a structured independent assessment pathway rather than blanket refusal — because refusing without offering a pathway abandons the patient and does not resolve the prescribing safety problem

zeroMarkTraps: Treating the GP's caution as an obstacle to the patient's care rather than appropriate clinical governance
Endorsing shared-care prescribing without independent assessment because the diagnosis has already been made
Framing the response primarily around managing Thomas's anger rather than addressing the prescribing safety issues in the referral
stems.postExaminerNote: Candidates who named each missing element with a specific patient-safety consequence, supported the GP's position explicitly, and offered a pathway rather than only a refusal scored in the upper range. Candidates who treated the online report as sufficient basis for shared-care or who organised their answer around managing Thomas's anger rather than the referral's clinical deficiencies did not achieve the standard.

stems.vignette: Thomas attends the clinic demanding an immediate stimulant prescription. He brings a printed copy of the online clinic report and says the diagnosis has already been made and that further delay is obstructing his care. A junior registrar is sitting in and appears unsure whether to continue the interview or end it. The GP phones the clinic during the appointment asking for a same-day decision because Thomas has returned to the GP reception desk repeatedly.
stems.question:
Describe — list and explain how you would manage this consultation, including your engagement with Thomas, supervision of the registrar, response to the GP, and maintenance of safe prescribing boundaries.
A list without explanation will not be awarded any marks.
(10 marks | 10 minutes)
stemSignals: This consultation is simultaneously a clinical encounter, a supervision task and a GP communication task — treating it as only one of these misses the consultant register the question is testing
The registrar's uncertainty is itself a governance signal — a junior clinician who is unsure whether to end the interview needs explicit real-time guidance, not a debrief after the consultation
The GP's phone call during the appointment creates pressure for a same-day decision — this must be managed without making a prescribing commitment under duress

domains.name: Consultation Management (4 marks)
keyPoints: Acknowledge Thomas's frustration without validating his conclusion that the diagnosis is established and prescribing should follow — because alliance requires hearing his distress while maintaining clinical boundaries

Explain the clinical reason for independent assessment calmly and specifically — because Thomas deserves to understand that stimulants can worsen mania, psychosis, anxiety and cardiovascular risk, and that the online report does not contain the information needed to prescribe safely

Clarify that complaint rights are respected but do not determine prescribing decisions — because Thomas needs to understand that his right to complain is acknowledged, but that this is separate from the clinical decision
Offer a timely structured assessment pathway as the alternative to immediate prescribing — because refusing without a pathway creates therapeutic abandonment and escalates the consultation

domains.name: Registrar Supervision (2 marks)
keyPoints: Give the registrar explicit in-consultation direction — because a junior clinician who is uncertain whether to continue an interview is in a situation where real-time guidance protects both the patient and the registrar

Debrief the registrar after the consultation about managing prescribing pressure and boundary-setting — because the registrar needs to understand that safety overrides complaint threat and why this is modelled by the consultant

domains.name: GP Response and Documentation (2 marks)
keyPoints: Respond to the GP by end of day with a clinical summary and proposed assessment pathway rather than a prescribing decision — because same-day GP communication supports the GP safely and prevents Thomas from returning to the GP reception desk with a misrepresentation of what occurred

Document Thomas's demands, the clinical explanation provided, the risk assessment, and the plan — because contemporaneous documentation protects both the patient and the clinical team if a complaint follows

domains.name: Risk Assessment (2 marks)
keyPoints: Conduct a focused risk assessment during the consultation — because escalating behaviour, anger and impulsivity require assessment of aggression, self-harm, intoxication and substance use before the consultation closes

Note any signs of intoxication, withdrawal, manic or psychotic features observed during the interview — because these directly affect safety and may indicate why urgent independent assessment is needed

zeroMarkTraps: Ending the consultation early or asking Thomas to leave without offering a clinical pathway
Providing the GP with a same-day prescribing commitment to manage reception desk disruption
Treating the registrar's uncertainty as a post-consultation debrief matter rather than a real-time supervision need
stems.postExaminerNote: Candidates who addressed all three parallel tasks — patient engagement, registrar supervision and GP response — with specific actions for each, and who maintained prescribing boundaries without abandoning the therapeutic relationship scored in the upper range. Candidates who treated this as only a de-escalation exercise, or who gave the GP a prescribing commitment to resolve the reception desk problem, did not achieve the standard.

stems.vignette: During your assessment, Thomas reports two previous admissions for elevated mood, decreased need for sleep, grandiosity and reckless spending. He also drinks heavily most nights and uses cocaine monthly. These details were not included in the online ADHD report. He says that was years ago and the only issue now is focus.
stems.question:
Discuss — list and debate your response to the GP's shared-care stimulant prescribing request in light of this new information, including your diagnostic formulation, alternative treatment priorities and any service-level governance action.
A list without debate specific to this case will not be awarded any marks.
(10 marks | 10 minutes)
stemSignals: Thomas's minimisation of his bipolar history is itself a clinical data point — years ago does not change the contraindication to unsequenced stimulant prescribing
The online report did not screen for mood disorder or substance use — this is not a minor omission; it is a systematic failure that directly created the current clinical risk

The governance action must address the pattern, not just this individual case — online ADHD clinic reports without mood or substance screening represent a service-boundary problem that requires a systemic response

domains.name: Diagnostic Formulation (3 marks)
keyPoints: Reformulate Thomas's presentation to prioritise bipolar disorder and alcohol and cocaine use disorder — because these diagnoses are not historical background; they are active clinical conditions that change the risk profile and treatment sequence

ADHD remains a possible comorbid diagnosis but cannot be safely assessed or treated until mood and substance use are stabilised — because stimulants may precipitate mania, worsen mixed states and interact with alcohol and cocaine

Thomas's minimisation that this was years ago does not reduce the contraindication — because the biological and prescribing risk of stimulants in bipolar disorder is not time-limited by the patient's subjective experience of stability

domains.name: Treatment Priorities (4 marks)
keyPoints: Decline the GP's shared-care stimulant prescribing request at this stage — because prescribing stimulants without mood stabilisation and substance-use assessment would be clinically indefensible

Communicate the refusal transparently and specifically to Thomas — because he deserves an honest explanation that this is a safety decision based on his medical history, not a judgment about his character or the legitimacy of his ADHD difficulties
Prioritise assessment and treatment of bipolar disorder including relapse prevention and mood stabilisation — because this is the highest-risk active condition in the formulation

Address alcohol and cocaine use with an AOD referral and motivational work — because substance use independently impairs cognition, complicates bipolar management, and must be treated before ADHD can be fairly assessed

Provide the GP with a written plan that specifies what would need to change before ADHD medication could be reconsidered — because the GP needs clear guidance for future shared-care conversations with Thomas

domains.name: Service Governance (3 marks)
keyPoints: Raise the pattern of online ADHD reports without mood or substance screening as a service-level governance issue — because this case is unlikely to be isolated; a systematic gap in private assessment quality requires a systemic response

Develop or contribute to a triage protocol for online ADHD assessment referrals — because the public service needs minimum assessment standards, triage criteria and GP guidance for handling these referrals

Document the clinical reasoning and prescribing refusal carefully — because if Thomas makes a complaint, the documentation must show that the refusal was based on identified safety risks, not administrative gatekeeping

zeroMarkTraps: Accepting Thomas's framing that the bipolar history was years ago and does not affect current prescribing
Prescribing stimulants to avoid a complaint despite active bipolar disorder and polysubstance use
Treating the governance response as optional rather than as a consultant obligation when a systemic referral pattern is identified
stems.postExaminerNote: Candidates who reformulated around bipolar disorder and substance use as the primary clinical priorities, declined stimulant prescribing with a specific safety rationale, and named a service-level governance response to the pattern of inadequate online reports scored in the upper range. Candidates who accepted the online diagnosis and proceeded to shared-care conditions, or who organised the refusal around administrative grounds rather than clinical safety, did not achieve the standard.

title: Daniel — ADHD, Methamphetamine Relapse and Stimulant Diversion
case: Daniel, 33, ADHD, methamphetamine dependence, stimulant diversion, heavy machinery operator
topic: High-risk stimulant prescribing in methamphetamine dependence; tests occupational and public-safety risk assessment before dose escalation, duties around confidentiality and workplace notification after confirmed diversion and relapse, and harm-minimisation recovery planning with return-to-work criteria

stems.vignette: Daniel is a 33-year-old warehouse supervisor with a childhood diagnosis of ADHD and a history of methamphetamine dependence. He reports four months of abstinence and is prescribed long-acting methylphenidate by a previous psychiatrist. He now asks for a dose increase and short-acting dexamphetamine for the afternoons. He operates heavy machinery at work and reports near-miss incidents, which he attributes to undertreated ADHD. He has poor sleep, increasing irritability and excoriations on his arms, which he describes as stress scratching.
stems.question:
Outline — list and justify the immediate clinical, occupational and public-safety risks you would assess before considering any change to Daniel's medication.
A list without because-clauses specific to this case will not be awarded any marks.
(8 marks | 8 minutes)
stemSignals: Daniel is attributing near-miss incidents to undertreated ADHD — this is a self-serving explanation that must be assessed against other explanations including relapse, stimulant toxicity and sleep deprivation before any dose change is considered
The excoriations, poor sleep and irritability form a clinical cluster that requires explanation before prescribing is changed — each has a different implication for safety and management
Operating heavy machinery is not background information — it creates a specific foreseeable harm to others that changes the urgency and nature of the safety assessment

domains.name: Clinical Risk Assessment (4 marks)
keyPoints: Assess for methamphetamine relapse — because the near-misses, poor sleep, irritability and excoriations are consistent with stimulant use or stimulant withdrawal and must be objectively investigated before any prescribed stimulant dose is increased
Assess for stimulant toxicity from the current methylphenidate dose — because restlessness, insomnia and irritability may indicate the current dose is already excessive rather than insufficient

Assess for psychosis or hypomanic features — because methamphetamine and stimulant medications can both precipitate psychotic and manic symptoms that could explain the near-miss incidents and behavioural changes
Review prescription monitoring records — because early replacement requests, dose escalation patterns or multiple prescribers would indicate diversion or misuse before the current consultation
Order a urine drug screen — because self-report of abstinence in a high-stakes prescribing context with physical signs of possible stimulant use requires objective verification

domains.name: Occupational and Public Safety (4 marks)
keyPoints: Assess fitness to operate heavy machinery immediately — because near-miss incidents in a person with possible stimulant intoxication or relapse create foreseeable risk to Daniel and to others in the workplace
Assess driving safety — because Daniel commutes to work and the same clinical concerns that affect machinery operation apply to driving

Do not increase stimulant medication before safety risks are assessed and resolved — because escalating prescribing in a person with possible relapse or stimulant toxicity may increase rather than reduce workplace risk
Explore what the employer knows about the near-miss incidents — because if workplace safety has already been compromised, there may be an obligation to act that goes beyond clinical monitoring

zeroMarkTraps: Accepting Daniel's self-attribution of near-misses to undertreated ADHD without independent assessment
Increasing the stimulant dose or adding dexamphetamine before the safety assessment is complete
Treating the excoriations as a minor incidental finding rather than a potential clinical signal requiring explanation
stems.postExaminerNote: Candidates who identified the near-misses as a workplace and public safety trigger requiring immediate assessment before any prescribing change, connected the clinical cluster of excoriations, insomnia and irritability to possible relapse or toxicity, and named objective verification via urine drug screen and prescription monitoring as essential steps scored in the upper range. Candidates who proceeded to a prescribing plan without addressing the safety assessment, or who accepted Daniel's explanation of near-misses without scrutiny, did not achieve the standard.

stems.vignette: Prescription monitoring records show two early replacement requests from emergency departments for lost medication. A urine drug screen is positive for methamphetamine. Daniel initially denies relapse, then admits he has been using methamphetamine on weekends and selling some prescribed tablets to repay a drug debt. He says please do not stop everything — if he loses his job he will have nothing.
stems.question:
Discuss — list and debate your duties regarding stimulant prescribing, confidentiality, workplace safety and fitness to operate machinery, given what you now know.
A list without debate specific to this case will not be awarded any marks.
(10 marks | 10 minutes)
stemSignals: Daniel's disclosure is a turning point — confirmed diversion, relapse and machinery operation while using methamphetamine create duties that extend beyond the therapeutic relationship

The tension between confidentiality and public safety is genuine and must be debated — Daniel's job loss is a real harm; but his current fitness to operate heavy machinery creates a foreseeable risk to others that confidentiality does not protect
Daniel's emotional appeal must be heard and acknowledged — but it cannot drive the prescribing decision; the clinical and legal obligations remain regardless of the consequences he fears

domains.name: Stimulant Prescribing (2 marks)
keyPoints: Suspend stimulant prescribing immediately — because confirmed methamphetamine use and diversion make continued stimulant prescribing clinically indefensible and legally unsafe

Frame suspension as a safety intervention rather than a punitive response — because maintaining therapeutic alliance is essential for harm minimisation and Daniel must understand the clinical rationale, not experience it as punishment

domains.name: Confidentiality and Disclosure (4 marks)
keyPoints: Confidentiality is not absolute when there is foreseeable serious risk to identifiable others — because Daniel operating heavy machinery while using methamphetamine and diverting stimulants creates a specific, foreseeable risk to other workers that may override his privacy interests

The preferred first step is to ask Daniel to self-disclose to his employer and take leave from safety-critical duties — because voluntary disclosure preserves autonomy and alliance while addressing the safety risk

If Daniel refuses to self-disclose and continues operating machinery, a limited breach of confidentiality to prevent foreseeable serious harm may be justified — because the duty to prevent harm to identified third parties can override confidentiality when the risk is serious, specific and not manageable by other means

Any disclosure must be minimum necessary — because the legal and ethical basis for overriding confidentiality requires that only the information needed to prevent the specific harm is shared, not the full clinical history

domains.name: Fitness to Drive and Operate Machinery (2 marks)
keyPoints: Advise Daniel that he is not fit to operate heavy machinery or drive while using methamphetamine — because this advice must be documented as given regardless of whether he accepts it

Follow applicable jurisdictional reporting obligations regarding fitness to drive — because obligations vary by jurisdiction but generally require documentation that the patient has been advised, and may require reporting if there is continued unsafe driving

domains.name: Ongoing Care (2 marks)
keyPoints: Maintain the clinical relationship and arrange addiction service involvement urgently — because discharging Daniel because he has diverted medication increases the risk of methamphetamine escalation, suicide and complete disengagement

Acknowledge Daniel's fear of job loss directly — because validating the real consequences he faces while maintaining clinical boundaries is both therapeutically necessary and models honest consultant communication

zeroMarkTraps: Continuing stimulant prescribing with increased monitoring after confirmed diversion and methamphetamine use
Treating confidentiality as absolute when Daniel continues operating heavy machinery while using methamphetamine
Discharging Daniel from the service because he has diverted medication
stems.postExaminerNote: Candidates who suspended prescribing with a safety rationale, debated confidentiality against public safety with a specific conclusion about what would justify disclosure, named voluntary self-disclosure as the preferred first step, and maintained the therapeutic relationship throughout scored in the upper range. Candidates who continued prescribing with additional conditions, treated confidentiality as absolute, or discharged Daniel punitively did not achieve the standard.

stems.vignette: Daniel agrees to take leave from work and engage with the addictions team. His employer sends a form requesting medical clearance before he can return to forklift operation. The addictions team says they will work with him but are concerned that untreated ADHD may undermine relapse prevention. Daniel asks what would need to change before ADHD medication could be reconsidered.
stems.question:
Describe — list and explain a harm-minimisation and recovery-oriented management plan, including addiction treatment, prescribing boundaries, monitoring and return-to-work criteria.
A list without explanation will not be awarded any marks.
(11 marks | 11 minutes)
stemSignals: The addictions team's concern about untreated ADHD is clinically valid — ADHD impulsivity and dysregulation genuinely increase relapse risk; non-stimulant ADHD options and psychosocial strategies must be considered as part of the recovery plan, not deferred indefinitely

The employer's medical clearance form requires a response — it must be handled with Daniel's consent and should address functional capacity, not disclose diagnoses or clinical history beyond what Daniel has agreed

Daniel's question about what would need to change to reconsider ADHD medication is a therapeutic opportunity — answering it clearly and honestly supports recovery orientation and gives Daniel a structured pathway rather than an indefinite prohibition

domains.name: Addiction Treatment and Recovery (3 marks)
keyPoints: Engage the addictions team as the primary treatment coordination service — because methamphetamine dependence with diversion requires specialist addiction management including relapse prevention, contingency planning and peer support

Address financial coercion from drug debt through social work and safety planning — because the drug debt that drove diversion represents an ongoing risk that addiction treatment alone will not resolve
