Develop a crisis plan for methamphetamine craving, relapse and self-harm risk — because each of these may emerge during early recovery and Daniel needs a clear response pathway

domains.name: ADHD Treatment During Recovery (3 marks)
keyPoints: Consider non-stimulant ADHD treatment during the recovery phase — because the addictions team's concern about untreated ADHD is valid; atomoxetine or other non-stimulant options may address ADHD without the diversion and relapse risk of stimulants

Offer ADHD-specific cognitive and behavioural strategies — because psychosocial approaches to executive functioning, impulsivity and planning have evidence in ADHD and directly support relapse prevention

Explain to Daniel the specific conditions under which stimulant prescribing could be reconsidered — because a clear, transparent pathway is more therapeutically effective than an open-ended refusal and supports recovery motivation

domains.name: Prescribing Boundaries and Monitoring (3 marks)
keyPoints: If stimulants are ever reconsidered, require demonstrated sustained abstinence verified by random urine drug screens — because prescribing stimulants to someone with active methamphetamine use or recent diversion recreates the original risk

Require single prescriber, single pharmacy and written prescribing agreement before any stimulant trial — because these governance safeguards must be in place before prescribing resumes, not added reactively

Establish prescription monitoring review as a standard component of any future stimulant trial — because the pattern of early replacement requests was the first objective evidence of diversion; this monitoring must continue

domains.name: Return-to-Work and Employer Response (2 marks)
keyPoints: Obtain an independent occupational assessment before providing medical clearance for forklift operation — because the clinician's role is not to certify fitness for a safety-critical role without objective functional assessment; an occupational physician or independent assessor should provide clearance

Any communication with the employer requires Daniel's explicit informed consent — because the employer's form must be completed with minimum necessary functional information agreed with Daniel, not a disclosure of diagnosis, treatment or substance use history

zeroMarkTraps: Prescribing stimulants as part of the recovery plan because Daniel is engaged with treatment
Providing the employer with a medical clearance without an independent occupational assessment
Deferring all ADHD treatment indefinitely rather than considering non-stimulant options during recovery
stems.postExaminerNote: Candidates who addressed the addictions team's concern about ADHD by naming non-stimulant options, gave Daniel a specific and transparent pathway for future stimulant reconsideration, required independent occupational assessment before employer clearance, and built monitoring into the prescribing governance plan scored in the upper range. Candidates who prescribed stimulants as part of the engagement offer, certified fitness without occupational assessment, or refused all ADHD treatment indefinitely did not achieve the standard.

title: Oliver — Child ADHD, Tics and Parental Conflict
case: Oliver, 10, ADHD, stimulant-related tics, school refusal, separated parents in conflict
topic: Child ADHD complicated by emerging tics, school refusal, and parental conflict involving shared parental responsibility; tests clinical appraisal of new movements and comorbidities, ethical management of adversarial parenting without losing focus on the child, and biopsychosocial planning when parents demand opposite outcomes

stems.vignette: Oliver is a 10-year-old boy diagnosed with ADHD six months ago by a paediatrician and taking modified-release methylphenidate. His mother brings him to clinic because over the past month he has developed frequent blinking, facial grimacing and shoulder shrugging. These movements worsen later in the day and when he is tired or embarrassed. Oliver is now refusing school. He says he hates school because everyone stares at him. The school has warned the family that he may be suspended due to emotional outbursts and classroom disruption.
stems.question:
Outline — list and justify your clinical appraisal of Oliver's new movements, school refusal and emerging emotional difficulties.
A list without because-clauses specific to this case will not be awarded any marks.
(8 marks | 8 minutes)
stemSignals: The movements must be characterised before they are attributed to methylphenidate — their timing, suppressibility, fluctuation and relationship to emotional state are diagnostically important
Oliver's voice is clinically significant — everyone stares at me is a direct account of the functional and social impact that must inform the formulation, not be noted as background

School refusal and emotional outbursts are not simply behavioural — they require explanation in terms of tic visibility, shame, anxiety, peer teasing and possibly medication timing before a management plan is formed

domains.name: Clinical Appraisal of Movements (3 marks)
keyPoints: Characterise the movements as possible tics — because the pattern of involuntary repetitive movements affecting face and shoulders that worsen with fatigue and embarrassment is consistent with a tic disorder, which may be primary or stimulant-related

Do not assume methylphenidate must be immediately stopped — because tics may be comorbid with ADHD independent of medication, may be dose-related and reversible, or may have emerged coincidentally; dose reduction, timing change or switching are options that require clinical assessment first

Review medication dose, timing and recent dose changes — because tic exacerbation associated with peak stimulant levels or a recent dose increase is different in implication and management from tics that occur independent of medication timing

domains.name: School Refusal and Emotional Functioning (3 marks)
keyPoints: Assess school refusal as a consequence of visible tic distress and peer teasing, not primarily as an ADHD behaviour problem — because the cause of avoidance determines the intervention; treating this as non-compliance misses the underlying shame and anxiety

Seek independent collateral from the school — because the school's warning of suspension must be understood in the context of whether staff have observed tics, teasing, and emotional dysregulation, which changes both the formulation and the school intervention needed

Assess anxiety as a comorbidity requiring active treatment — because anxiety worsening after lunch may reflect rebound from morning methylphenidate and is also an independent driver of school avoidance and emotional outbursts

domains.name: Oliver's Perspective and Functional Impact (2 marks)
keyPoints: Hear Oliver's account directly and give it clinical weight — because his description of being stared at is not simply a child's complaint; it describes the social consequence of visible tics that is driving school avoidance and requires a specific response

Assess the risk of continuing school exclusion — because suspension would remove Oliver from the educational environment at a time when academic and social development is disrupted, compounding the functional harm already present

zeroMarkTraps: Automatically stopping methylphenidate without characterising the movements or considering alternative explanations
Increasing the methylphenidate dose because behaviour is described as worse by the mother without first understanding what is driving the behaviour
Treating Oliver's school refusal as a behavioural problem without connecting it to tic visibility, shame and peer teasing
stems.postExaminerNote: Candidates who characterised the movements before attributing them to medication, gave clinical weight to Oliver's account of peer staring, and connected school refusal to tic-related shame and anxiety rather than ADHD non-compliance scored in the upper range. Candidates who stopped or increased medication without clinical appraisal, or who treated the school behaviour as the primary clinical problem without addressing what was driving it, did not achieve the standard.

stems.vignette: Oliver's parents are separated and share parental responsibility. His father contacts you stating he objects to psychiatric drugs and believes Oliver's mother is exaggerating symptoms to influence parenting arrangements. His mother says the father has never accepted Oliver's difficulties and that stopping medication would be educational neglect. Both parents ask you to put in writing that the other parent is acting against Oliver's best interests.
stems.question:
Discuss — list and debate the ethical, legal and communication issues in managing Oliver's care in the context of this parental conflict.
A list without debate specific to this case will not be awarded any marks.
(10 marks | 10 minutes)
stemSignals: Both parents are asking for adversarial letters — this request must be declined but the refusal must be explained in terms of Oliver's interests, not as a bureaucratic boundary

Shared parental responsibility has specific legal implications for consent to treatment — if parents cannot agree, there is a defined pathway that must be followed rather than allowing one parent's view to default
The clinical question and the family-court question are different — the clinician's role is to provide evidence-based care for Oliver, not to adjudicate the parenting dispute

domains.name: Oliver's Welfare as the Primary Frame (2 marks)
keyPoints: Keep Oliver's welfare as the central focus of every decision — because both parents are framing this as a dispute about each other; the clinician's role is to maintain focus on what Oliver needs clinically, not to validate either parent's position

Involve Oliver in decisions in a manner proportionate to his developmental capacity — because a 10-year-old has a voice in treatment decisions and his account of distress is clinically relevant independent of the parental conflict

domains.name: Consent and Shared Parental Responsibility (3 marks)
keyPoints: Clarify the legal consent position for treatment with shared parental responsibility — because both parents have legal standing in treatment decisions; clinical action requires either joint agreement or a court direction if agreement cannot be reached on a significant non-urgent treatment change

Seek joint parental agreement where possible — because unilateral clinical decisions in shared-care arrangements without attempting to engage both parents create legal exposure and damage the therapeutic relationship with the excluded parent

Escalate to legal or ethics advice if parental disagreement prevents necessary treatment for Oliver — because the clinician should not be forced into a position of choosing between parents; a formal mechanism exists for resolving this

domains.name: Declining Adversarial Letters (2 marks)
keyPoints: Decline to write adversarial letters against either parent — because the clinician's role is not to provide advocacy material for family-court proceedings; doing so compromises neutrality, creates medico-legal risk, and harms the therapeutic relationship

Explain the refusal in terms of Oliver's interests rather than as a refusal to engage — because framing the refusal as protecting Oliver from being placed in the middle of a parental dispute is more likely to maintain both parents' engagement than a simple boundary statement

domains.name: Documentation and Communication (3 marks)
keyPoints: Document both parents' views, Oliver's clinical presentation, and the independent school collateral separately from the parental dispute — because clinical records must reflect objective clinical findings and all relevant perspectives, not become a record of the parental conflict

Use objective school data to anchor clinical decisions — because independent school collateral about tic visibility, peer teasing and academic functioning provides a basis for clinical recommendations that is not dependent on either parent's account
Communicate the same clinical information to both parents — because differential communication to parents in conflict creates further division and exposes the clinician to claims of bias

zeroMarkTraps: Writing an adversarial letter for either parent because the clinical evidence supports one parent's position
Allowing one parent's legal threat to determine the treatment decision
Treating shared parental responsibility as permission for either parent to make unilateral treatment decisions
stems.postExaminerNote: Candidates who kept Oliver's welfare as the explicit primary frame, declined adversarial letters with a child-focused explanation, addressed consent under shared parental responsibility, and used independent school data to anchor clinical recommendations scored in the upper range. Candidates who wrote letters supporting one parent's position, or who allowed legal threats to determine the clinical decision, did not achieve the standard.

stems.vignette: Independent school collateral confirms that Oliver's tics are visible and distressing, that peers tease him, and that his anxiety worsens after lunch. His attention improves on medication but tics and anxiety appear worse since the dose increase three months ago. His mother fears academic collapse if medication is reduced; his father demands immediate cessation.
stems.question:
Describe — list and explain a biopsychosocial management plan addressing medication, tics, anxiety, school functioning and the parental disagreement.
A list without explanation will not be awarded any marks.
(10 marks | 10 minutes)
stemSignals: The school collateral has changed the clinical picture — it is now independently confirmed that tics are visible and causing peer teasing, which is driving school avoidance; this must drive the plan, not either parent's demand

The dose increase three months ago is clinically significant — tic and anxiety worsening correlated with dose escalation suggests a dose-timing or dose-level problem that should be addressed before any other change
The parents' demands are opposite — the plan must be child-centred and evidence-based, not a compromise between the parents' positions

domains.name: Medication Plan (3 marks)
keyPoints: Consider dose reduction or timing adjustment as the first medication step — because tics and anxiety worsened after the dose increase; reverting toward the previous dose or adjusting timing to reduce peak-level effects addresses the most likely medication contributor without stopping treatment entirely

Monitor attention, tics, anxiety and appetite after any medication change — because the clinical goal is to find the lowest dose that maintains acceptable attention benefit without worsening tics or anxiety

If tics remain distressing despite optimised stimulant dosing, consider a specialist review for tic-specific treatment options — because severe or functionally impairing tics that persist on optimised stimulant dosing may warrant a trial of a medication that addresses both ADHD and tics

domains.name: Tics and Anxiety (3 marks)
keyPoints: Provide psychoeducation to Oliver and both parents about tic disorders — because demystifying the movements and explaining they are not voluntary or a sign of psychiatric severity reduces shame and improves parental collaboration

Offer tic-specific behavioural strategies if available — because habit reversal training and Comprehensive Behavioural Intervention for Tics have evidence in this age group and address the tics directly without medication change

Provide child-focused anxiety treatment — because anxiety worsening in the afternoons requires active intervention; CBT for anxiety in children is evidence-based and addresses the social anxiety component driving school avoidance

domains.name: School Functioning (2 marks)
keyPoints: Convene a school meeting to address teasing, provide Oliver with safe spaces and adjust classroom expectations during the transition — because the school's threat of suspension must be redirected toward an anti-bullying and adjustment support plan for Oliver
Provide graded return-to-school planning if school avoidance has become established — because abrupt return without addressing the teasing and anxiety that drove avoidance is likely to fail

domains.name: Parental Disagreement (2 marks)
keyPoints: Present the plan to both parents simultaneously with the same written information — because a child-centred evidence-based plan presented equally to both parents is less easily contested than separate communications

Name the basis for clinical recommendations as independent school data and Oliver's account — because anchoring recommendations in objective evidence reduces the likelihood that either parent can successfully frame the plan as favouring the other

zeroMarkTraps: Stopping methylphenidate immediately because the father demands it
Continuing the current dose because the mother fears academic collapse
Writing a plan that is a compromise between the parents' demands rather than a clinically justified child-centred response
stems.postExaminerNote: Candidates who used independent school collateral as the clinical anchor, addressed the dose-timing relationship to tic and anxiety worsening before any other change, provided both parents the same evidence-based plan without adversarial framing, and included school-level intervention beyond individual treatment scored in the upper range. Candidates whose plan was primarily responsive to parental demands rather than Oliver's clinical needs, or who automatically stopped or increased medication without a clinical rationale, did not achieve the standard.

title: Sophie — ADHD Treatment During Pregnancy
case: Sophie, 24, ADHD in pregnancy, abrupt stimulant cessation, perinatal service
topic: Perinatal ADHD management after GP-advised abrupt stimulant cessation; tests risk-benefit analysis of treating versus withholding ADHD medication during pregnancy, informed shared decision-making with a distressed pregnant patient, and perinatal service governance when no clinician will accept prescribing responsibility

stems.vignette: Sophie is a 24-year-old laboratory scientist who is 10 weeks pregnant. The pregnancy was unplanned but is now wanted. She has a longstanding diagnosis of ADHD and has been stable for several years on lisdexamfetamine. When her pregnancy was confirmed, her GP advised her to stop the medication immediately because stimulants are unsafe in pregnancy. Since stopping, Sophie has become disorganised, sleep-deprived and distressed. She has made several errors at work, forgotten meals and appointments, and left the stove on twice. She reports shame, hopelessness and passive suicidal thoughts.
stems.question:
Discuss — list and debate the decision to abruptly cease Sophie's stimulant medication, including the maternal, fetal, occupational and safety considerations.
A list without debate specific to this case will not be awarded any marks.
(9 marks | 9 minutes)
stemSignals: The GP's decision to stop all stimulants immediately was not a safe default — abrupt cessation without specialist review created the current clinical crisis; the examiner trap is framing this as GP versus medication rather than as inadequate individualised risk-benefit analysis
Passive suicidal thoughts in the context of hopelessness and functional collapse are a safety signal that must be assessed immediately — they are not background distress

Sophie's occupational errors in a laboratory setting and domestic safety failures are not incidental — they represent real-world functional impairment that creates risk to her, the pregnancy and potentially others

domains.name: Critique of the Abrupt Cessation Decision (3 marks)
keyPoints: Abrupt cessation without specialist review was not standard care — because medication decisions in pregnancy require individualised risk-benefit analysis that weighs fetal exposure risk against maternal risk from untreated illness, not a blanket prohibition

Blanket stimulant cessation advice oversimplifies a genuinely uncertain evidence base — because available evidence suggests stimulant risks in pregnancy are real but context-dependent; a discussion of options including lowest effective dose, medication-free periods, or non-pharmacological alternatives would have been more appropriate than immediate cessation

The GP's intention to reduce fetal risk is understandable — but the outcome has been functional collapse, occupational errors and suicidal ideation, all of which create risk to the pregnancy that the cessation was intended to protect

domains.name: Maternal Risk from Untreated ADHD (3 marks)
keyPoints: Untreated ADHD in pregnancy creates specific risks beyond subjective distress — because disorganisation, missed meals, sleep deprivation and domestic safety failures are not minor functional inconveniences; they represent concrete maternal and fetal risks

Passive suicidal ideation requires urgent safety assessment — because hopelessness and passive ideation in a pregnant woman who feels she is failing and is ashamed require immediate clinical attention, not management as a secondary issue

Occupational errors in a laboratory setting may create risk to Sophie, other staff or products — because the functional consequences of untreated ADHD extend beyond the home environment and may create an occupational safety dimension

domains.name: Fetal Considerations (2 marks)
keyPoints: Fetal exposure risk from stimulants must be acknowledged honestly — because the evidence base is limited and fetal risks including possible effects on growth and cardiovascular parameters are real, even if their magnitude is uncertain

There is no zero-risk option — because both continued medication exposure and the risks created by severe untreated maternal ADHD carry fetal implications; honest engagement with this uncertainty is required for genuine informed consent

domains.name: Immediate Safety Response (1 mark)
keyPoints: Conduct an urgent safety assessment for passive suicidal ideation before any treatment decision — because suicidal thoughts in the context of acute functional deterioration must be assessed for risk level, plan, access to means and protective factors before any other clinical priority is addressed

zeroMarkTraps: Endorsing the GP's decision as a reasonable precaution without engaging with the clinical consequences of abrupt cessation
Treating passive suicidal ideation as background distress rather than as a safety signal requiring immediate assessment
Framing the clinical task as choosing between medication safety and fetal safety rather than as an individualised risk-benefit analysis
stems.postExaminerNote: Candidates who critiqued the abrupt cessation on clinical grounds rather than on pharmacological grounds alone, named the maternal risks from untreated ADHD as equally real to fetal medication risks, conducted an immediate safety assessment for suicidal ideation, and framed the task as individualised risk-benefit analysis rather than a binary choice scored in the upper range. Candidates who endorsed the GP's decision, or who discussed fetal pharmacology without addressing Sophie's acute functional and safety deterioration, did not achieve the standard.

stems.vignette: Sophie attends with her partner, who says they want the baby to be safe but that Sophie is falling apart and they need honest information rather than blanket reassurance. Sophie asks whether restarting medication would make her a bad mother. She is also worried her employer will dismiss her if she makes another laboratory error.
stems.question:
Describe — list and explain the information and communication approach you would use to support informed shared decision-making about ADHD treatment during pregnancy.
A list without explanation will not be awarded any marks.
(9 marks | 9 minutes)
stemSignals: Sophie's question about being a bad mother is the most clinically important statement in this stem — it signals shame-driven reasoning that will interfere with genuine informed consent if not addressed directly

The partner's request for honest information rather than blanket reassurance is a legitimate clinical request — it requires a communication approach that acknowledges uncertainty rather than providing false certainty in either direction
The occupational concern is not separate from the clinical decision — Sophie's ability to continue working safely is part of the risk-benefit analysis and connects treatment to real-world functioning

domains.name: Addressing Shame and Alliance (2 marks)
keyPoints: Address Sophie's shame directly and before providing clinical information — because her question about being a bad mother reflects a belief that needing treatment is a moral failure; this belief must be explicitly corrected for genuine informed consent to be possible

Name that treatment during pregnancy is not a moral position — because needing psychiatric medication during pregnancy is a clinical decision, not a character assessment; framing it this way creates the conditions for Sophie to engage with the actual risks and benefits

domains.name: Communicating Uncertainty Honestly (3 marks)
keyPoints: Explain that the evidence on stimulants in pregnancy is genuinely limited and that certainty in either direction is not appropriate — because both reassurance that stimulants are safe and prohibition on the grounds that they are definitely harmful misrepresent the evidence; honest engagement with uncertainty is the correct clinical standard

Describe the known and possible fetal risks from stimulant exposure in accessible terms — because Sophie and her partner need to understand what is known, what is uncertain and what monitoring can address, not a pharmacology lecture

Describe the maternal and fetal risks from severe untreated ADHD in equal terms — because informed decision-making requires both sides of the risk-benefit analysis to be presented with equal clarity and without either being minimised

domains.name: Decision-Making Process (2 marks)
keyPoints: Include Sophie's partner as a participant in the informed consent discussion — because the partner has directly requested honest information and their engagement supports Sophie's decision-making and reduces the isolation that shame creates

Document the shared decision-making discussion explicitly — because in a pregnant patient with suicidal ideation and a contested medication decision, contemporaneous documentation of what information was given, what Sophie understood, and what she decided protects both Sophie and the clinical team

domains.name: Occupational and Practical Considerations (2 marks)
keyPoints: Discuss the occupational dimension as part of the risk-benefit analysis — because Sophie's ability to work safely in a laboratory is not separate from her clinical situation; it is a concrete risk that medication or non-medication management must address

Explore interim non-pharmacological strategies that could reduce occupational and domestic risk during the decision-making period — because Sophie may need time to decide and immediate supports can reduce harm regardless of the medication decision

zeroMarkTraps: Providing a pharmacology information session about stimulants in pregnancy without addressing Sophie's shame or her question about being a bad mother
Offering false certainty in either direction about stimulant safety in pregnancy
Excluding the partner from the informed consent discussion despite their explicit request for honest information
stems.postExaminerNote: Candidates who addressed shame before providing clinical information, communicated uncertainty honestly without false reassurance in either direction, included the partner as a named participant in the decision-making process, and connected the occupational safety concern to the risk-benefit analysis scored in the upper range. Candidates who began with a pharmacology review, gave blanket reassurance or prohibition, or treated the partner as background scored lower.

stems.vignette: After discussion, Sophie wishes to restart medication at the lowest effective dose. Her previous private psychiatrist has retired and no clinician has accepted ongoing prescribing responsibility. The GP will only prescribe if the perinatal service provides written governance and monitoring instructions. Obstetrics requests a risk-benefit plan, and Sophie's employer requests guidance about temporary work adjustments.
stems.question:
Discuss — list and debate whether the perinatal service should accept interim prescribing responsibility, and describe the multidisciplinary management plan including monitoring, non-pharmacological supports, workplace liaison and postpartum planning.
A list without debate and explanation will not be awarded any marks.
(11 marks | 11 minutes)
stemSignals: The prescribing responsibility question is the core dilemma of this final stem — if no clinician accepts responsibility, Sophie remains untreated because of system fragmentation, not because treatment is contraindicated
The GP has offered a conditional willingness to prescribe — this is a shared-care opportunity that should be actively developed rather than declined
Multiple external parties are now requesting guidance simultaneously — the plan must assign specific responsibilities to each party rather than producing a generic multidisciplinary statement

domains.name: Prescribing Responsibility Debate (3 marks)
keyPoints: The perinatal service should consider accepting interim prescribing responsibility — because if no other clinician will prescribe and treatment is clinically indicated, Sophie will be left untreated by system fragmentation rather than clinical contraindication; this is a foreseeable harm the perinatal service has an obligation to address

Accepting prescribing responsibility should be time-limited with a documented transition plan — because interim prescribing in a specialist service should include a planned handover to a community psychiatrist or GP shared-care arrangement rather than becoming indefinite

The GP's conditional willingness to prescribe creates a shared-care pathway that should be actively developed — because providing the GP with written governance and monitoring instructions transforms their conditional offer into an active prescribing arrangement, which is the most sustainable long-term model

domains.name: Monitoring During Pregnancy (2 marks)
keyPoints: Coordinate with obstetrics for fetal growth monitoring and maternal blood pressure and cardiovascular surveillance — because stimulant effects on maternal blood pressure and fetal growth require obstetric input that the psychiatry service cannot provide independently

Establish monitoring of maternal weight, appetite, sleep, anxiety and mood throughout pregnancy — because the same parameters that matter for stimulant safety in non-pregnant adults remain relevant in pregnancy with additional attention to nutritional adequacy

domains.name: Non-Pharmacological Supports (2 marks)
keyPoints: Establish partner-supported routines, environmental reminders and task management strategies to reduce functional impairment during the titration period — because non-pharmacological supports reduce the dose needed and provide a safety net if medication needs to be adjusted

Develop a crisis and safety plan that includes rapid clinical review if suicidal ideation worsens, sleep deteriorates significantly or functional safety errors recur — because the suicidal ideation documented in the first stem requires an ongoing monitoring and response plan

domains.name: Workplace and Employer Liaison (2 marks)
keyPoints: Liaise with Sophie's employer only with her explicit informed consent — because any communication with the employer must be limited to what Sophie has agreed to share and should address functional adjustment needs rather than diagnoses or treatment details

Recommend temporary adjustments to high-risk laboratory tasks during the stabilisation period — because the occupational errors that occurred during untreated ADHD created a safety risk that temporary task modification can address without requiring full disclosure of diagnosis

domains.name: Postpartum Planning (2 marks)
keyPoints: Plan postpartum psychiatric follow-up before perinatal discharge — because sleep deprivation in the postpartum period worsens ADHD and mood, and a clinician needs to be named before the perinatal service closes the episode

Address breastfeeding and stimulant prescribing as a postpartum decision that requires informed consent before delivery — because breastfeeding plans affect prescribing choices and should be discussed proactively rather than urgently after birth

zeroMarkTraps: Declining perinatal service prescribing responsibility on administrative grounds when no other clinician will prescribe and treatment is clinically indicated
Providing generic multidisciplinary recommendations without assigning specific monitoring responsibilities to obstetrics, psychiatry and the GP
Failing to include postpartum planning as a named component of the perinatal management plan
stems.postExaminerNote: Candidates who debated the prescribing responsibility question explicitly and concluded that service fragmentation leaving Sophie untreated is a foreseeable harm requiring action, developed the GP's conditional offer into a shared-care plan, assigned specific monitoring responsibilities to each party, and included postpartum planning before perinatal discharge scored in the upper range. Candidates who declined prescribing on administrative grounds, or who produced a generic multidisciplinary plan without assigned responsibilities, did not achieve the standard.

title: James — Clozapine Initiation, Monitoring and Governance
case: James, 24, treatment-resistant schizophrenia, involuntary admission, clozapine initiation
topic: Clozapine initiation in treatment-resistant schizophrenia; tests CPMS registration and baseline requirements before first dose, risk-benefit communication with a capacitous patient and anxious mother addressing non-haematological side effects, safe titration under bed pressure during the myocarditis window, and consultant response to a formal complaint with monitoring failure

title: Sarah — Clozapine Side-Effect Burden, Autonomy and Recovery
case: Sarah, 38, treatment-resistant schizophrenia, stable on clozapine, request to stop, community clinic
topic: Clozapine side-effect burden in a stable community patient who requests cessation; tests assessment before responding to the request, ethical and legal response to a capacitous patient asking about her right to stop including compulsory treatment limitations, identification of the drug interaction and lifestyle factors driving elevated clozapine levels, and integration of clozapine treatment with vocational recovery goals

title: Andrew — Clozapine Toxicity, Medical Ward and Discharge Governance
case: Andrew, 46, schizophrenia on clozapine 8 years, respiratory admission, toxicity from smoking cessation and polypharmacy
topic: Clozapine toxicity on a medical ward driven by smoking cessation, fluvoxamine interaction and sedating polypharmacy; tests differential formulation prioritising smoking cessation as the primary driver, clinical advice to the respiratory team on managing clozapine during acute infection, safe management of agitation without adding high-dose antipsychotics to an elevated clozapine level, and discharge governance for a patient who will resume smoking

title: Robert — Missed Clozapine Doses and Transfer-of-Care Failure
case: Robert, 52, schizophrenia stable on clozapine 12 years, missed doses after discharge, ED re-presentation, system failure
topic: Clozapine missed doses after discharge due to monitoring system failure; tests immediate clinical risk assessment before prescribing, disposition decision under bed pressure when re-titration is required, open disclosure to an angry family member, and governance response to a serious medication safety near-miss including registrar supervision and systems redesign

title: William — CTO Renewal, Predictive Risk and Side Effects
case: William, 42, schizophrenia, 18 months on CTO, third cycle of apparent stability, side effects, Tribunal review

title: Nathan — Akathisia, Depot Refusal and Dynamic Risk Under CTO
case: Nathan, 27, schizophrenia, early psychosis team, depot refusal, likely akathisia, CTO, father's warning

title: Jack — Rural CTO Transfer Failure and Predictive Risk
case: Jack, 36, schizoaffective disorder, rural transfer, CTO, depot due, no documentation, no stock, no nurse
